rulings.Case Title: ROSA ANN ROBINSON VS. JEFFERY WINDHAM ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Rosa Robinson'S Motion By Plaintiff For Assignment Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 2, Plaintiff Rosa Robinson's Motion For Assignment Order. Plaintiff Rosa Ann Robinson's motion for assignment order is granted in the following amounts. Defendants Jeffery Windham and JeffWindham and Associates, LLC are ordered to assign to plaintiff Robinson 15% of monies due or which may be come due to either of them for driving services related to Lyft or Uber, and 25% of monies due or which may become due to them related to real estate services or the purchase or sale of real property or sale of contracts related to real property. This order shall serve as the assignment. Payments subject to this order shall be made to John F. Bradley, Jr., Esq., 28 North First Street, Suite 400, San Jose California 95113-1210. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order repeating the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: EDWARD A BYRD JR VS. TURNER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Defendant'S Employee Joel Kellogg And Designated Person Most Knowledgable In Compliance With Notice Of Deposition And Production Of Documents And For Sanctions, (Per Ost Order Filed 6-18-19)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 1, Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Defendant'S Employee Joel Kellogg And Designated Person Most Knowledgable In Compliance With Notice Of Deposition And Production Of Documents And For Sanctions, (Per Ost Order Filed 6-18-19). Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion DENIED. There is no indication that the parties have reached an impasse regarding the scheduling of the deposition such that Court involvement is necessary. The parties should meet-and-confer about the scheduling of the deposition. The request for sanctions is DENIED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EDWARD A BYRD JR VS. TURNER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plantiff'S Motion To Compel Defendant' Compliance With The Court'S May 10, 2019 Orders And For Sanctions. (Per Ost Order Filed On 6-18-19)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 1, Plantiff'S Motion To Compel Defendant' Compliance With The Court'S May 10, 2019 Orders And For Sanctions. (Per Ost Order Filed On 6-18-19). Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is DENIED. While the discovery responses ordered by the May 10, 2019, order were originally unverified, which may be treated as non-responsive, verifications were provided and I decline to exercise my discretion to sanction the delay. The sanctions ordered in the May 10 Order were conditional: if Defendant provided the ordered discovery by the time ordered, the sanctions would not be imposed, if not, the sanctions were due. This was intended to incentivize timely compliance. Other than with respect to the verification issue discussed above, Defendants complied with the order, and I conclude this was substantially compliant such that the sanctions were not triggered. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS STEFAN VS. KAREN SIOU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Karen Sious Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT KAREN SIOU's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant Karen Siou's summary judgment motion is granted. Plaintiff alleges that defendant orally agreed to "[c]ollaborate on producing a written psychological diagnosis" and breached the agreement by not doing so. I grant summary judgment first because plaintiff failed to file a separate statement as required. (CCP §437c(b)(3).) Moreover, plaintiff states that defendant "kept wavering back and forth" and "at no time did we have an explicit agreement." (Def. Ex. F 25-26, 32, 33.) Thus, the alleged agreement was not definite enough to be enforced. (Ladas v. California State Automobile Assn. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 770.) Plaintiff's inadmissible and irrelevant "evidence" creates no triable issue as to existence of an oral agreement. I deny plaintiff's belated request to amend the complaint. Plaintiff noticed no such a motion, the request is untimely and prejudicial given next month's trial date, and the request appears to be a sham given plaintiff's discovery admissions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: LAWRENCE S. DORFMAN VS. RICHARD CARTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Appointing A Partition Referee Per Ccp 873.010
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF LAWRENCE DORFMAN's Motion Appointing A Partition Referee Per CCP 873.010. The joint report filed by counsel on July 23, 2019 is not what the court ordered. The July 23 report cursorily describes a failed meet-and-confer. The court ordered a report "stating each side's views of individual referee candidates." Thus, the parties are ordered to file, on or before August 2, a new joint report that sets out in three separate headings the name of each candidate. Immediately following the heading, the party advocating the candidate is to describe why he or she should be appointed. Following that, the party opposing the appointment is to describe why the candidate should not be appointed. The same format is to be used for each of the three candidates. Resumes may be attached to the joint report. The hearing on appointing a partition referee is continued to August 9, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: OFA JUSTIN TAIMANI VS. CALIFORNIA FLEET MAINTENANCE INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Subpoenas And Request For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF OFA TAIMANI'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas And Request For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: It was not a "reasonable and good faith attempt to resolve informally any dispute concerning discovery," CCP 2023.010(f), for Plaintiff to send a meet-and-confer letter, receive a substantive response, and then "decline[] to respond." MPA at 4. Similarly, as Defendant points out, CRC 3.1345 requires a separate statement to support a motion to quash a subpoena - particularly when the motion relates to the content of the subpoena. Failure to file a separate statement supports denying the motion. Mills v. U.S. Bank (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 871, 892. Either of these failures by Plaintiff would support denying the motion. However, considering the privacy issues implicated, and Defendant's willingness to consider more tailored subpoenas, the motion to quash is GRANTED. Defendant is permitted to serve revised subpoenas that comport with the offer set out in the Cho letter of April 22, 2019. The parties are free to agree to a stipulation for a protective order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SEBASTIEN SIKORSKI VS. ELIZABETH PHILLIPS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Defendant To Answer Plaintiffs Interrogatory And Notice Of Hearing (Part 3 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 7, Motion To Compel Defendant To Answer Plaintiffs Interrogatory And Notice Of Hearing (Part 3 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling). (Continued from Part 2 of Tentative Ruling) Interrogatory 21 and 25: Hearing required. The court does not see what documents would help defendant answer these interrogatories. Interrogatory 26: Granted. Defendant shall answer this interrogatory. But before defendant has to answer, plaintiff shall produce to defendant the statements referred to in this interrogatory. See ruling on 12 above. Interrogatory 27: Granted. Defendant shall answer this interrogatory. But before defendant has to answer, plaintiff shall produce to defendant all documents in his possession showing defendant paid on behalf of plaintiff, the amount of $2,446 to a third party, a seller of skydiving equipment.. See ruling on 12 above. Interrogatory 29: The interrogatory asks defendant for what efforts, if any, she made to preserve and/or retrieve access to her email accounts from the relevant period. The answer she gave did not respond to what the interrogatory asks. She must answer if she made any efforts to preserve and/or retrieve access to her email accounts from the relevant period. If she made no efforts, then her answer is "None." If she made efforts, then she must state what efforts she made. Hearing required as to how much time plaintiff and defendant need to comply with the order. The court will reserve ruling on sanctions to see if defendant answers the interrogatories in compliance with this order. Plaintiff's request to deem defendant a hostile witness without credibility is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highmanlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the the tentative ruling.) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SEBASTIEN SIKORSKI VS. ELIZABETH PHILLIPS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Defendant To Answer Plaintiffs Interrogatory And Notice Of Hearing (Part 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 7, Motion To Compel Defendant To Answer Plaintiffs Interrogatory And Notice Of Hearing (Part 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of Tentative Ruling) Interrogatory 9: Granted. In answering an interrogatory, defendant has a duty to make a reasonable search for the information if she doesn't know the answer. A reasonable search of FINRA online records would have revealed the answer to this interrogatory. Interrogatory 10: Granted. Defendant did not answer the interrogatory as to whether she was a party to the arbitration. The answer would be, "Yes," "No," or "I don't know." But defendant should not answer "I don't know" unless she has made a reasonable and good faith effort to obtain the information and after making that effort, still doesn't know. The court notes that it can't tell from the record plaintiff attached as an exhibit if defendant was a party to the arbitration or whether the only party was Schwab, albeit with it being alleged that defendant was the Schwab employee who committed the wrongful acts. Interrogatory 11: Hearing required. The parties shall bring a copy of the complaint and amended complaints with exhibits to the hearing for the court to look at. Defendant obviously has copies of the exhibits since the complaint and amended complaint were served on her. The court tentatively doesn't understand what more defendant needs to see before she can state whether she disputes the authenticity of any of the exhibits. Interrogatory 12: Hearing required. Granted. Defendant shall answer this interrogatory. But before she has to do so, the court believes it fair that defendant be provided with documents in plaintiff's possession to refresh her memory before having to answer this interrogatory. (See Rosemont v. Superior Court (1964) 60 Cal.2d 709; Poeschl v. Superior Court (1964) 229 Cal.App.2d 383.) Therefore, before defendant has to answer this interrogatory, plaintiff shall produce to her all documents in his possession, custody or control showing defendant solicited plaintiff by presenting various trading opportunities and detailed examples of her highly successful trades. Interrogatory 13: Granted. Plaintiff wrote "Defendant" in this interrogatory when he obviously meant "Plaintiff." The court therefore corrects this typo so the interrogatory says "Plaintiff" rather than "Defendant." As so corrected, Defendant shall answer the interrogatory. Interrogatories 14 and 16: Granted. Defendant shall answer these interrogatories. Interrogatories 15 and 22-24: Granted. Defendant shall answer this interrogatory. But before defendant has to answer, plaintiff shall produce to defendant the emails referred to in these interrogatories. See ruling on 12 above. Interrogatories 17 and 19: Granted. Defendant shall answer these interrogatories. But before defendant has to answer, plaintiff shall produce to defendant all written requests in his possession, custody or control that are referred to in these interrogatories. See ruling on 12 above. Interrogatory 18: Granted. Defendant shall answer this interrogatory. But before defendant has to answer, plaintiff shall produce to defendant the report referred to in this interrogatory, as well as any written documents he received from defendant prior to January 2013 which provide a precise value of the trading account. See ruling on 12 above. Interrogatory 20: Granted. Defendant shall answer this interrogatory. But before defendant has to answer, plaintiff shall produce to defendant the statement and requests referred to in this interrogatory. See ruling on 12 above. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SEBASTIEN SIKORSKI VS. ELIZABETH PHILLIPS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Defendant To Answer Plaintiffs Interrogatory And Notice Of Hearing
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Friday, July 26, 2019, Line 7, PLAINTIFF SEBASTIEN SIKORSKI'S Motion To Compel Defendant To Answer Plaintiffs Interrogatory. (Part 1 of Tentative Ruling) This matter is specially set to be heard at 2:00 p.m. by Judge Pro Tem Bruce Highman. Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted in part. Hearing required. The motion was not late. Per the proofs of service, it was originally served on June 19. Then a file-endorsed copy was served on June 28. Service on June 19 was timely. Moreover, even if the motion were served late, there is no evidence Ms. Phillips was prejudiced by the late service. She got in her opposition. Defendant must verify her interrogatory answers under penalty of perjury. The verification is typically placed on a separate page at the end of the interrogatory answers. Under Code of Civil Procedure 2015.5, the verification should say: "I declare under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California that the foregoing interrogatory answers are true and correct, and that this declaration was executed on ____________, 2019." If defendant wants to qualify or otherwise modify or change any of her answers now that she has been ordered to verify them under penalty of perjury, she may do so. Interrogatory 2: Defendant shall answer a), b) and c) of this interrogatory. Interrogatory 4: Hearing required. Defendant answered by providing her address from 2002 through 2019. In light of what plaintiff says the process server told him, the court wants to ask Ms. Phillips if the address she disclosed is her current address. Interrogatory 5: Denied. The interrogatory is a special interrogatory. It is close to Judicial Council Form Interrogatory 2.6 but not identical. Special interrogatories can't contain subparts if a party objects. The court construes defendant's response to (b) "Please rephrase as question" as an objection. Interrogatory 7: Granted. Defendant shall answer a), b), and c) of this interrogatory. Interrogatory 8: Granted. Although defendant is obviously in pro per, she will answer as to whether she is currently incurring legal fees for a lawyer to help her. (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2) =(302/JPT)

If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel and for sanctions is DENIED. Based on the communications between counsel, Defendant reasonably should have known there would be a minor delay in service of discovery responses and could have waited to file this motion. CCP 2023.010(f). Moreover, the responses appear to have been provided. I find there was good cause for the delay because of the substitution of counsel. Objections are not waived. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is also DENIED. While I find good cause for the delay, the responses were served late. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
